Q1: I tried to keep a consistent study routine, usually studying about three hours a day spread across the week. Being a kinesthetic learner, I found it hard to just sit and read, so I'd often study while doing something hands-on like making flashcards or walking around my room reciting concepts. I'd block out time in the mornings when my mind felt clearest, though honestly my attendance wasn't great because sometimes I just studied better on my own at home.
Q2: My pattern definitely varied depending on what was due. I aimed for regularity, but when assignments piled up or I had seven online courses demanding attention, things got chaotic. Deadlines would make me cram more intensely, but between deadlines I tried to maintain at least some daily engagement with the material. It wasn't perfect, but I made it work with what time and energy I had.
Q3: Usually I logged in when I needed to check assignment deadlines or watch lecture videos I'd missed from not attending class. The gamified tasks actually got me logging in more regularly because I wanted to see my progress and earn those achievement badges. Sometimes I'd get notification emails reminding me about discussions, and that would prompt me to check in even when I wasn't planning to.
Q4: Yes, after a few weeks the gamified elements really hooked me into a routine. I started checking in almost daily just to see if there were new challenges or if my ranking had changed. It became like checking social media—just a quick habit. The points system made it feel less like a chore and more like something I was building toward.
Q5: The video lectures were incredibly useful because I could pause, rewind, and physically practice concepts while watching. I spent over three and a half hours on videos because I'd often watch them multiple times while doing hands-on exercises. The discussion forums were also helpful because explaining things to peers helped me understand concepts better myself. The gamified tasks made completing work feel rewarding rather than just obligatory.
Q6: I often skipped the supplementary reading materials because they felt too passive for my learning style. I need to do something with the information, not just read it. Some of the optional quizzes I also delayed because they didn't count toward my grade and I was already stretched thin managing seven courses with limited resources.
Q7: An activity felt worth my time when I could see immediate results or progress. The gamified elements were perfect for this—I could see points accumulating and badges unlocking. Activities that let me practice and apply concepts rather than just absorb information passively also felt valuable. If I couldn't connect it to my assignments or exams, it was harder to justify spending time on it.
Q8: Optional activities felt like a luxury I couldn't always afford. With seven online courses and limited resources, I had to prioritize ruthlessly. Required activities got done no matter what because my grades depended on them. Optional ones I'd only tackle if I had extra energy or if the gamification made them feel fun rather than like extra work.
Q9: Absolutely, I did a lot of studying that never showed up in the LMS. I'd download lecture slides and videos to study offline since my internet isn't always reliable. I made physical flashcards and practice problems that I'd work through while pacing around my room. Sometimes I'd study with classmates in person, quizzing each other verbally, which helped me learn but left no digital trace.
Q10: There were many times I was deeply engaged with course material but the LMS wouldn't show it. I'd print out problem sets and work through them by hand at the library. I'd record myself explaining concepts and listen back while doing chores. My best studying often happened away from screens because I learn by doing, not by clicking through online modules.
Q11: Yes, frequently. I'd watch a video once online, then download it or take detailed notes that I'd review repeatedly offline. With my internet being unreliable sometimes, I learned early to grab materials when I could and then study them independently. One viewing in the system might represent hours of offline review for me.
Q12: There were definitely harder moments, especially when I felt scared about whether I was keeping up with everything. Managing seven courses with low resources meant constant anxiety about falling behind. When assignments from multiple courses overlapped, I'd feel overwhelmed and sometimes just freeze instead of engaging. My attendance dropped during those periods because going to class felt less efficient than studying independently.
Q13: Definitely. When I was fatigued or confused, I'd log into the LMS but not really absorb anything. Competing responsibilities from other courses meant I sometimes had to skim materials rather than engage deeply. With limited resources, I couldn't always access materials when I needed them most, which affected my engagement patterns significantly.
Q14: Yes, there were times I'd open videos or readings but my mind was elsewhere, worried about other deadlines or just exhausted. I'd technically be logged in and clicking through, but not really learning. Those sessions felt like going through the motions rather than genuine engagement. My anxiety would spike and I'd just stare at the screen without processing anything.
Q15: I postponed discussion posts sometimes because they required more mental energy than I had available. Even knowing they were important for my learning and grade, when I was scared or overwhelmed, writing coherent responses felt impossible. I'd tell myself I'd do it later when I felt more capable, but later often meant last minute.
Q16: Deadlines completely shaped my LMS usage. I'd have bursts of intense activity right before things were due, logging in multiple times to check requirements and submit work. Between deadlines, my engagement was steadier but less urgent. The gamified tasks helped smooth this out somewhat because they gave me ongoing goals beyond just assignment deadlines.
Q17: Feedback definitely affected my engagement. When I got positive feedback, it motivated me to engage more and try harder on the next assignment. Negative or confusing feedback sometimes made me anxious and hesitant to dive back in. I appreciated quick feedback because waiting made my anxiety worse and I'd avoid the course until I knew where I stood.
Q18: The course structure with gamified elements really helped my participation. Having clear milestones and visible progress kept me coming back even when motivation was low. The structured deadlines gave me external accountability I needed. However, when the structure was too rigid and didn't account for my other six courses, it became stressful rather than helpful.
Q19: The gamification made engagement much easier—it turned studying into something almost enjoyable. The clear organization of materials helped too since I could find what I needed quickly. What made it harder was when there were too many small tasks spread across the week; with limited time and resources, I couldn't always keep up with everything the way the course expected.
Q20: I think that could happen when someone is genuinely trying but struggling to produce quality work. They might be reviewing materials repeatedly, attempting assignments multiple times, or just anxious and checking things obsessively without completing submissions. High activity doesn't always mean productive activity—sometimes it's just anxiety manifesting as constant checking.
Q21: This actually reflects my experience somewhat. I performed well on exams and got a Distinction despite moderate attendance because I studied extensively offline. My kinesthetic learning style meant I often learned better through hands-on practice away from the computer. The LMS activity doesn't capture the hours I spent with physical notes and practice problems.
Q22: Course logs can accurately show when and how often someone logs in, what they click on, and submission patterns. But they miss the quality of engagement—whether someone was actually thinking deeply or just scrolling mindlessly. They miss offline studying, emotional state, and the real effort behind each interaction. My best learning often happened away from the trackable system.
Q23: Being engaged means I'm actively thinking about the material, connecting it to things I already know, and wanting to understand rather than just complete tasks. It's when I'm curious and motivated, not just going through motions. For me, true engagement often involves physical activity—writing, moving, practicing—not just passive consumption of content.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show how scared I often felt, or how much effort it took to maintain good performance with limited resources and seven courses. It wouldn't capture my offline study sessions, my physical flashcards, or the peer discussions I had outside the platform. It also wouldn't show that despite low motivation, my resilience kept me pushing through. The emotional labor of being a student doesn't leave digital traces.
